channel.title: Texts on emsenn.net
channel.description: Recent content in Texts on emsenn.net

item.title: How We Measure the Universe
item.description:
How do you measure the distance to something you cannot visit? You use a ladder.

The problem

Distance is the most fundamental measurement in cosmology, and the hardest to make. You cannot stretch a tape measure to a galaxy. You cannot bounce a radar signal off something billions of light-years away. Every technique for measuring cosmic distances relies on indirect methods, each with its own assumptions and limitations.

Rung 1: Parallax

The first rung is geometry. As Earth orbits the Sun, nearby stars appear to shift slightly against the background of distant stars. This apparent shift is parallax. The parallax angle gives the distance directly, by trigonometry, with no assumptions about the star’s properties.

item.title: The CMB as Evidence
item.description:
In 1965, Arno Penzias and Robert Wilson were testing a microwave antenna at Bell Labs and found a persistent noise they could not eliminate. It came from every direction. It did not vary with the time of day or the season. They had discovered the cosmic microwave background.

The prediction

The CMB was predicted before it was found. In the 1940s, George Gamow, Ralph Alpher, and Robert Herman reasoned that if the universe began in a hot, dense state, the radiation from that early epoch should still be present, cooled by the expansion of space. They estimated a present-day temperature of a few kelvin. The prediction was largely forgotten until Penzias and Wilson’s accidental discovery, which earned them the 1978 Nobel Prize.
